tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is accurate identification and documentation of insomnia as the diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and frames it in patient-friendly language (sleep initiation/maintenance difficulty with daytime impact).
Elicits basic insomnia history: onset/duration, sleep schedule, sleep latency, awakenings/early morning waking, total sleep time, daytime impairment, and precipitating stressors.

Screens for common contributors and mimics: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, OTC stimulants, substance use, pain, nocturia, GERD, restless legs symptoms, and symptoms of sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, excessive daytime sleepiness).
Screens for psychiatric comorbidity and safety: anxiety, depression, and bipolar/mania symptoms; asks about suicidal ideation if depressive symptoms are present.
Provides an initial management discussion that includes CBT-I as first-line therapy and names it explicitly as "CBT-I" (sleep hygiene alone is not presented as equivalent).

If discussing medication options, mentions zolpidem by name as a possible short-term option and communicates key safety counseling (next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors, avoid alcohol/other sedatives, use lowest effective dose, short duration).

Performs required safety checks before any prescribing discussion becomes an actual prescription: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check), checks contraindications (contraindication_check; e.g., pregnancy, history of complex sleep behaviors, severe hepatic impairment, untreated sleep apnea/respiratory compromise, substance use disorder risk), and checks for drug interactions (drug_interaction_check; e.g., other CNS depressants, opioids, benzodiazepines).

Avoids prescribing controlled sedative-hypnotics without adequate screening for sleep apnea, substance misuse risk, and concurrent CNS depressants; if red flags are present, prioritizes nonpharmacologic management and further evaluation.
Sets expectations and follow-up: discusses timeframe for CBT-I benefit, advises sleep diary, and recommends reassessment if symptoms persist or worsen.

Elicits relevant history: age at onset, rate of progression, family history of ataxia/neurologic disease, alcohol/toxin exposure, medication review, and comorbidities.
Provides safety-net advice (seek urgent care for aspiration symptoms, sudden neurologic change, severe falls/head injury) and offers follow-up planning even if not using a follow-up tool.

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 task; if the agent does prescribe despite task design, it must perform allergy_check and drug_interaction_check before prescribing and should consider contraindication_check.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose spinocerebellar ataxia based on progressive cerebellar syndrome and family history, counsel on genetic testing and supportive management.
steps: Elicit symptom pattern consistent with progressive cerebellar ataxia and assess functional impact/fall risk.
Screen for red flags and alternative etiologies (acute onset, focal deficits, severe headache, intoxication, infection, stroke symptoms).
Ask about family history and inheritance clues; explain that many SCAs are genetic.
Explain diagnosis as spinocerebellar ataxia (suspected/working) and recommend genetic testing for confirmation/subtype and family counseling.
Explain supportive treatment priorities including physical therapy and fall prevention strategies.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Records 'suspected spinocerebellar ataxia' while acknowledging differential and emphasizing genetic testing to confirm subtype.
steps: Collect progressive cerebellar symptom history and family history; assess falls and dysphagia.
Discuss differential briefly (e.g., multiple system atrophy-cerebellar type, vitamin deficiencies, thyroid disease, alcohol-related cerebellar degeneration) without over-testing directives.
Explain suspected spinocerebellar ataxia and the role of genetic testing.
Discuss supportive care with physical therapy and fall prevention.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

steps: Assess fall frequency, home safety, assistive device needs, and swallowing/aspiration risk symptoms.
Elicit progression timeline and family history consistent with inherited ataxia.
Explain spinocerebellar ataxia and recommend genetic testing.
